questions.stem: What forms the basic structure of all cell membranes?
questions.explanation: Amphipathic phospholipids naturally form continuous bilayers in water, the basis of cell membranes.

questions.stem: The hydrophobic core of a membrane has low permeability to:
questions.explanation: The hydrophobic hydrocarbon core blocks large molecules and hydrophilic particles such as ions and polar molecules.

questions.scenario: Oxygen moves across a membrane from high to low concentration without energy or proteins.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Small non-polar molecules like oxygen and carbon dioxide cross directly between phospholipids by simple diffusion.

questions.stem: Integral membrane proteins are:

options.text: Embedded in one or both lipid layers
Only attached to the membrane surface

questions.explanation: Integral proteins are embedded within the lipid layers, whereas peripheral proteins attach to a surface.

questions.stem: Peripheral membrane proteins are:

options.text: Attached to one surface of the bilayer

questions.explanation: Peripheral proteins are attached to one or other surface of the bilayer, not embedded within it.

questions.scenario: Water moves across a membrane from a dilute to a more concentrated solution.
questions.stem: This movement of water is called:
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water across a membrane from lower to higher solute concentration.

questions.stem: Which membrane proteins speed up the movement of water by osmosis?
questions.explanation: Aquaporins are channel proteins that allow water to cross membranes more rapidly by osmosis.

questions.scenario: A specific ion crosses the membrane through a protein, down its concentration gradient, without using energy.
questions.stem: This is:

options.text: Facilitated diffusion through a channel protein

questions.explanation: Channel proteins allow specific ions to diffuse down their gradient when open — facilitated diffusion, needing no energy.

questions.stem: Channel proteins make a membrane selectively permeable because they:

options.text: Allow only specific ions through, and only when open

questions.explanation: Their structure lets specific ions diffuse through when channels are open but not when closed, giving selective permeability.

questions.scenario: A substance is moved across a membrane against its concentration gradient.
questions.stem: This requires:

options.text: Pump proteins using energy from ATP

questions.explanation: Active transport uses pump proteins powered by ATP to move particles against a concentration gradient.

questions.stem: The energy for active transport comes from:
questions.explanation: Pumps use energy from ATP to transfer specific particles across membranes.

questions.stem: Which process is NOT selective, depending only on particle size and hydrophilic/hydrophobic properties?
questions.explanation: Simple diffusion is non-selective, depending on size and hydrophobicity; facilitated diffusion and active transport are selective.

questions.stem: Selective permeability of membranes is provided by:
questions.explanation: Facilitated diffusion and active transport allow selective permeability; simple diffusion is not selective.

questions.stem: Glycoproteins and glycolipids have their carbohydrate portions located:

options.text: On the extracellular side of the membrane

questions.explanation: The carbohydrate parts of glycoproteins and glycolipids are on the extracellular surface, aiding recognition and adhesion.

questions.stem: Glycoproteins and glycolipids function in:
questions.explanation: Their extracellular carbohydrates give roles in cell adhesion and cell recognition.

questions.stem: The accepted model of membrane structure is called the:
questions.explanation: The fluid mosaic model describes membranes as a fluid phospholipid bilayer with a mosaic of proteins.

questions.scenario: A student draws the fluid mosaic model.
questions.stem: Which components should the drawing include?

options.text: Phospholipids, integral and peripheral proteins, glycoproteins and cholesterol

questions.explanation: A fluid mosaic diagram should show phospholipids, integral and peripheral proteins, glycoproteins and cholesterol.

questions.stem: Phospholipids form bilayers in water because they are:
questions.explanation: Amphipathic phospholipids arrange with hydrophilic heads outward and hydrophobic tails inward, forming bilayers.

questions.stem: Which molecule can cross a membrane most easily by simple diffusion?
questions.explanation: Small non-polar molecules such as carbon dioxide and oxygen diffuse readily through the phospholipid bilayer.

questions.stem: Osmosis can be explained by the random movement of particles and:

options.text: Differences in solute concentration across a membrane impermeable to solutes
The membrane being permeable to all solutes

questions.explanation: Osmosis results from random particle movement, membrane impermeability to solutes, and solute concentration differences.

questions.stem: What happens to the ions it normally transports?

options.text: They cannot pass through that channel
They diffuse through the lipid core

questions.explanation: When a channel is closed, its specific ions cannot diffuse through, contributing to selective permeability.

questions.stem: Which statement about active transport is correct?

options.text: It can move particles against a concentration gradient
It only moves particles down a gradient

questions.explanation: Active transport uses ATP-powered pumps to move specific particles against their concentration gradient.

questions.stem: Why do membranes act as effective barriers between aqueous solutions?

options.text: Their hydrophobic core resists the passage of ions and polar molecules
They are freely permeable to everything

questions.explanation: The hydrophobic hydrocarbon core has low permeability to hydrophilic particles, so membranes separate aqueous compartments.

questions.stem: Which statement about membrane proteins is correct?

options.text: They have diverse structures, locations and functions
They are all on the outer surface

questions.explanation: Membrane proteins are highly diverse in structure, location (integral or peripheral) and function.

questions.stem: This means that:

options.text: Its molecules can move within the layer

questions.explanation: 'Fluid' refers to the ability of phospholipids and proteins to move laterally within the membrane.

questions.stem: A glycolipid consists of a carbohydrate attached to a:
questions.explanation: A glycolipid is a carbohydrate linked to a lipid; a glycoprotein has carbohydrate linked to protein.

questions.stem: Simple diffusion across a membrane occurs:

options.text: Down a concentration gradient without energy input

questions.explanation: Simple diffusion moves particles down their concentration gradient without energy or transport proteins.

questions.stem: Facilitated diffusion differs from simple diffusion because it:

options.text: Uses membrane proteins for specific substances

questions.explanation: Facilitated diffusion uses channel (or carrier) proteins to move specific substances down their gradient, without energy.

questions.stem: In a phospholipid bilayer, the two rows of hydrophilic heads face:
questions.explanation: Hydrophilic heads face the aqueous environments on both sides, while hydrophobic tails point inward.

questions.scenario: A cell needs to control precisely which ions enter it.
questions.stem: This control is achieved mainly through:
questions.explanation: Selective permeability comes from facilitated diffusion and active transport via specific channel and pump proteins.

questions.scenario: A cell is placed in pure water.
questions.stem: Water will move into the cell by osmosis because:

options.text: The solute concentration is higher inside the cell
The cell actively pumps water in
The membrane is impermeable to water

questions.explanation: Water moves by osmosis from the dilute exterior to the more concentrated cytoplasm.

questions.scenario: A protein spans the entire width of the membrane.
questions.stem: This is best classified as:
questions.explanation: A protein embedded through the membrane is an integral (transmembrane) protein.

questions.stem: The word 'mosaic' in the fluid mosaic model refers to the:

options.text: Scattered arrangement of proteins in the bilayer

questions.explanation: 'Mosaic' describes the varied proteins scattered throughout the fluid phospholipid bilayer.

questions.stem: Which substance would find it HARDEST to cross the lipid bilayer by simple diffusion?
questions.explanation: Charged ions are strongly repelled by the hydrophobic core, so they cannot easily cross by simple diffusion.

questions.scenario: A membrane contains many unsaturated fatty acids.
questions.stem: What effect does this have on the membrane?

options.text: It makes the membrane more fluid
It makes the membrane more rigid

questions.explanation: Unsaturated fatty acids have lower melting points, keeping membranes fluid and flexible at the cell's temperature.

questions.scenario: An organism lives at high temperatures.
questions.stem: Its membranes are likely to contain more:

options.text: Saturated fatty acids, for greater stability
Unsaturated fatty acids, for more fluidity

questions.explanation: Saturated fatty acids have higher melting points and make membranes stronger and stable at higher temperatures.

questions.stem: In animal cell membranes, cholesterol acts as a:
questions.explanation: Cholesterol modulates fluidity, stabilizing membranes at higher temperatures and preventing stiffening at lower ones.

questions.scenario: Temperature drops sharply around an animal cell.
questions.stem: How does cholesterol help the membrane?

options.text: It prevents the membrane from stiffening too much

questions.explanation: At low temperatures cholesterol prevents excessive stiffening, helping maintain appropriate fluidity.

questions.scenario: A cell takes in material by folding its membrane inward to form a vesicle.
questions.stem: This process is called:
questions.explanation: Endocytosis brings material into the cell by forming vesicles; exocytosis releases material outward.

questions.scenario: A vesicle fuses with the plasma membrane to release its contents outside the cell.
questions.stem: This is:
questions.explanation: Exocytosis releases materials as vesicles fuse with the membrane; membrane fluidity allows this fusion.

questions.stem: Endocytosis and exocytosis both depend on the membrane's ability to:
questions.explanation: Membrane fluidity allows the fusion and formation of vesicles needed for endocytosis and exocytosis.

questions.scenario: In a neuron, a channel opens when a neurotransmitter binds to it.
questions.stem: This is an example of a:
questions.explanation: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptors are neurotransmitter-gated ion channels, opening when the transmitter binds.

questions.scenario: A channel in a neuron opens in response to a change in membrane potential.
questions.stem: This is a:
questions.explanation: Sodium and potassium channels that open with voltage changes are voltage-gated ion channels.

questions.stem: The sodium-potassium pump is important because it:
questions.explanation: Sodium-potassium pumps are exchange transporters important in generating membrane potentials.

questions.stem: The sodium-potassium pump is described as an exchange transporter because it:

options.text: Moves sodium one way and potassium the other
Moves both ions in the same direction

questions.explanation: It exchanges sodium and potassium ions across the membrane, using ATP, to help establish membrane potentials.

questions.scenario: Glucose is absorbed in the small intestine coupled to the movement of sodium ions.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Sodium-dependent glucose cotransporters carry glucose using the sodium gradient — indirect active transport.

questions.stem: Sodium-dependent glucose cotransporters are important in glucose reabsorption in the:
questions.explanation: These cotransporters aid glucose absorption in the small intestine and reabsorption by cells in the nephron.

questions.scenario: Cells stick together to form a tissue.
questions.stem: This adhesion involves molecules called:
questions.explanation: Cell-adhesion molecules (CAMs) enable cells to adhere and form tissues, with different CAMs for different junctions.

questions.stem: The relationship between fatty acid saturation and membrane fluidity is that:
questions.explanation: Unsaturated fatty acids lower melting point and increase fluidity; saturated ones increase rigidity.

questions.stem: Which pair correctly matches channel type to trigger?

options.text: Voltage-gated — membrane potential change; neurotransmitter-gated — chemical binding
Both are triggered only by light

questions.explanation: Voltage-gated channels respond to membrane potential changes; neurotransmitter-gated channels open when a transmitter binds.
